  • princess-ux
  • life-admin
Princess Storybook
areas/career/work/princess-storybook.md
degree 3

Status: Active work project page. Current priority: scratch build; references only, not base. Active signals: reusable foundations/components/source-backed patterns; local fonts, not Princess/Typekit runtime URLs. Open loops: continue Storybook build from save

No resolved wikilinks.

Princess Storybook

Dashboard summary

  • Status: Active work project page.
  • Current priority: scratch build; references only, not base.
  • Active signals: reusable foundations/components/source-backed patterns; local fonts, not Princess/Typekit runtime URLs.
  • Open loops: continue Storybook build from saved plan.
  • Next concrete move: preserve Storybook-specific decisions here.

Migrated source: plans/princess-storybook-scratch-build.md

Princess Storybook Scratch Build Plan

Decision

Build a custom Princess Storybook from scratch. Use Storybook Design System, React USWDS, Carbon, and Material Design as references/north stars, not as source bases.

Why

  • Storybook Design System is too small to be the base.
  • React USWDS is useful for accessibility/form discipline but too government-specific.
  • Carbon is useful for enterprise completeness but too IBM/product-system-specific.
  • Material Design is useful for interaction principles, motion, accessibility, responsive layout, density, and component behavior patterns, but too Google/Android-specific to own as the Princess design language.
  • Princess needs clean reusable foundations/components first; specific work-project concepts belong outside the design-system Storybook unless later generalized into approved, reusable patterns.

North-star references

  1. Storybook Design System
  • lightweight docs/story organization
  • package structure
  • Chromatic/testing pattern
  1. React USWDS
  • accessibility discipline
  • form components
  • validation states
  • API/prop documentation
  1. Carbon
  • enterprise completeness
  • state/variant depth
  • component coverage model
  • mature design-system documentation
  1. Material Design
  • interaction principles and component behavior patterns
  • motion and transition guidance
  • accessibility and responsive layout guidance
  • density, spacing, navigation, and form-behavior references
  • use as reference only; do not import Google/Android visual identity into Princess

Build strategy

Phase 0: Repo setup

  • Create a fresh repo/project, likely /opt/data/projects/princess-storybook.
  • Use React + Vite + Storybook.
  • Add TypeScript.
  • Add CSS variables for tokens first.
  • Add Style Dictionary later only if token complexity warrants it.
  • Create private GitHub repo and backup automation for the new base.

Phase 1: Foundations

Create docs/stories for:

  • color
  • typography
  • spacing
  • radius
  • elevation/shadow
  • breakpoints/responsive behavior
  • evidence labels: confirmed, inferred, proposed, placeholder

Phase 2: Core components

Build first components:

  1. Button
  2. Input/Form Field
  3. Alert/Notification

Each component must include:

  • default state
  • hover/focus/active/disabled where relevant
  • error/loading where relevant
  • accessibility notes
  • usage guidance
  • evidence label
  • Figma/source reference when available

Phase 3: Source-backed reusable patterns

Do not use the design system as a home for specific work projects. Project-specific explorations, Jira/workstream synthesis, and decision-question surfaces belong in the wiki, Figma exploration files, or separate prototypes.

Only add patterns to Storybook when they are generalized into reusable design-system guidance and can be labeled with source confidence.

Phase 4: Preview/sharing

Short-term:

  • local Storybook dev server
  • temporary tunnel only when needed

Better longer-term:

  • Chromatic for Storybook review/versioning, or
  • Cloudflare Pages / Vercel / Netlify static Storybook, with access control if needed

Phase 5: Backup

  • Private GitHub repo as canonical backup.
  • Script-only scheduled backup job, no AI/model cost.
  • Do not backup node_modules, build outputs, secrets, or generated caches.

Definition of useful v0

The first useful version exists when Jenffry can open a preview and see:

  • foundations page
  • Button component
  • Input/Form Field component
  • Alert/Notification component
  • no project-specific workstream patterns
  • clear labels showing what is Princess source/refined vs inferred/proposed/temporary scaffold
  • enough quality that the artifact helps design thinking rather than becoming admin soup

2026-05-15 setup status

Completed:

  • Removed old local clone at /opt/data/projects/princess-storybook-design-system.
  • Removed old clone backup script and removed old cron job f1ea41a336d3.
  • Created fresh project at /opt/data/projects/princess-storybook using React + Vite + TypeScript + Storybook 10.
  • Added initial token layer in src/styles/tokens.css.
  • Added foundations stories:
  • src/foundations/Overview.stories.tsx
  • src/foundations/Tokens.stories.tsx
  • Added core components:
  • Button
  • Form Field
  • Alert
  • Initially added a payment/booking failure pattern, then later removed it after correcting the system boundary: specific project/workstream explorations do not belong in the design-system Storybook.
  • Verified npm run build-storybook succeeds.
  • Verified npm run build succeeds.
  • Started Storybook dev server and verified HTTP 200 locally.
  • Created temporary Cloudflare preview tunnel: https://generators-drilling-sand-publish.trycloudflare.com.
  • Created private GitHub repo: jenffry90-arch/princess-storybook.
  • Pushed initial commits to main.
  • Created new script-only backup job Princess Storybook GitHub backup (90d5cf918b5e) every 30 minutes using /opt/data/home/scripts/princess_storybook_git_backup.sh.

Current caveat: all tokens/visuals are placeholder until Princess Figma/production sources are audited.

2026-05-15 backup/credit-interruption check

Follow-up after Jenffry saw a credit-limit interruption and a failed cron backup alert.

Findings:

  • Project files were intact at /opt/data/projects/princess-storybook.
  • Git state was clean: local main matched origin/main at commit d8880418ab1f.
  • GitHub private repo jenffry90-arch/princess-storybook was reachable and synced.
  • npm run build succeeded.
  • npm run build-storybook succeeded.
  • The failed backup alert was caused by script path mismatch: cron resolves relative scripts under /opt/data/scripts/, but the script had been written only to /opt/data/home/scripts/.
  • Fix applied: copied backup script to /opt/data/scripts/princess_storybook_git_backup.sh, verified it targets /opt/data/projects/princess-storybook, and updated cron job 90d5cf918b5e to continue using princess_storybook_git_backup.sh.
  • Manual backup script run completed with no changes needed, and authenticated remote check confirmed local and GitHub main both at d8880418ab1f.

Status: no work appears lost from the interruption; backup path issue is fixed. The cron job's last error field may continue to show the old failure until the scheduler records the next successful run.

2026-05-15 Figma access preparation

Jenffry provided a Figma API token for later Princess Figma access work.

Security handling:

  • Token value is stored only in /opt/data/.env as FIGMA_TOKEN.
  • /opt/data/.env permissions verified/set to 600.
  • The token value is not recorded in the wiki, logs, GitHub, or project files.

Next use: build a Figma access helper or integration that reads FIGMA_TOKEN from the environment, then audit Princess tokens/components from Figma when the relevant file IDs are available.

  • Updated Button, Form Field, and Alert stories to expose evidence/source status.

  • Verified npm run build-storybook succeeds.
  • Committed and pushed to GitHub at 599c3e4 with message Add Storybook evidence layer.

2026-05-15 source-confidence labels clarified

Jenffry reviewed the evidence layer and confirmed the layer is useful, but the original labels felt too tied to the earlier clone/prune direction. Updated the language to fit a scratch-built system.

New displayed labels:

  • Princess source — directly verified from Princess Figma, production UI, documentation, or approved product decision.
  • Princess refined — based on real Princess source, then cleaned up for Storybook reuse/documentation.
  • Work inference — built from observed Princess work patterns or travel-commerce logic, not yet official.
  • UX proposal — Sapho/UX recommendation for discussion, not current Princess behavior.
  • Temporary scaffold — structural stand-in until real Princess evidence is added.

Completed:

  • Renamed the Storybook foundation page from Evidence System framing to Source Confidence framing.
  • Updated badge display labels and descriptions.
  • Updated component/story descriptions to use Source confidence language.
  • Verified npm run build and npm run build-storybook both succeed.
  • Committed and pushed to GitHub at 929a1bd with message Clarify Storybook source confidence labels.

2026-05-15 Material Design added as reference

Jenffry added Material Design as another reference for the Princess Storybook/design-system effort.

Decision:

  • Use Material Design as a reference/north star, not as a base and not as the Princess visual language.
  • Best use: interaction principles, motion, accessibility, responsive layout, density, navigation, and component behavior patterns.
  • Caution: avoid importing Google/Android visual identity or treating Material components as Princess-approved components.

Jenffry corrected the operating principle: the design system is not the place where specific work projects live.

  • Removed src/patterns/PaymentFailure/ and its evidence metadata because it was functioning as a project-specific decision surface rather than a reusable design-system primitive.

Current principle: project-specific synthesis belongs in the wiki or separate prototypes; Storybook gets reusable components and generalized patterns only.

2026-05-15 Figma MCP configured

Configured Hermes native MCP server figma using the existing local Figma token without storing the token value in config.

Configuration approach:

  • Config path: /opt/data/config.yaml
  • MCP server name: figma
  • Server package: figma-developer-mcp via npx
  • Auth source: reads /opt/data/.env, maps FIGMA_TOKEN to FIGMA_API_KEY inside the MCP subprocess
  • Secret handling: token value is not written to config, wiki, GitHub, or project files
  • Sampling disabled for this MCP server

Verification:

  • hermes mcp list shows figma enabled.
  • hermes mcp test figma connects successfully and discovers 2 tools: get_figma_data and download_figma_images.

Operational note: gateway/current sessions need restart or /reload-mcp/new session before Figma MCP tools appear in Sapho's live tool list.

2026-05-16 Typography web font rendering correction

Jenffry flagged that Storybook was not using the right typographies.

Root cause:

  • The Figma token/PDF language uses the human-readable names Domaine Display and Proxima Nova.
  • The actual Princess web CSS exposes those fonts to the browser as DomaineDisplay and proxima-nova.
  • Storybook had the correct source style values, but the browser font-family names were wrong, so it could fall back to Georgia/system sans.

  • Updated scripts/generate-tokens.mjs so --pc-font-family-display generates as "DomaineDisplay", "Domaine Display", Georgia, "Times New Roman", serif.

  • Added @import url('https://use.typekit.net/mvj6ajr.css') for Princess proxima-nova loading.

  • Ran npm run tokens:build so src/styles/tokens.css reflects the corrected generated family names.

2026-05-16 Semantic component tokens added

Jenffry asked whether Storybook was tokenized so a token change would reflect across the design system, then asked Sapho to make that true.

Completed:

  • Added src/styles/semantic-tokens.css and imported it after generated Figma foundation tokens in src/index.css.
  • Created semantic token groups for:
  • Storybook visualization: --pc-viz-*
  • shared display cards: --pc-card-*
  • buttons: --pc-button-*
  • form fields: --pc-field-*
  • alerts/notifications: --pc-alert-*
  • evidence/source-confidence UI: --pc-evidence-*
  • Refactored Button.css, FormField.css, Alert.css, and Evidence.css to consume semantic tokens instead of scattering source values through component CSS.
  • Kept exact source-backed values where needed, but promoted them into semantic tokens so future changes happen from one place.

Standing rule for all future components:

  1. New components must define semantic component tokens before or alongside visual CSS.
  2. Component CSS should consume semantic tokens, not raw colors/spacing/radius/elevation values, except for temporary scaffolds that are explicitly labeled and converted before source-backed promotion.
  3. Exact Figma/source values are allowed, but they belong in the semantic token layer, not scattered through component rules.
  4. Reusable Storybook display surfaces must keep using --pc-viz-* / --pc-card-* tokens so the Storybook itself remains a design-system surface.
  5. During component review, tokenization is part of done: if changing a token would not update the component globally, the component is not fully integrated.
  • Verified npm run build and npm run build-storybook pass.
  • Committed and pushed 7a86baa with message Add semantic component tokens.

areas/career/work/princess-ux.md

CareerCurrent StrategyOpen Loops

Princess UX

Dashboard summary

  • Status: Active work project context.
  • Current priority: keep Princess UX/UI context available without mixing Jira with personal TickTick clutter.
  • Active signals: UX/UI work clusters, Princess planning, AI/workflow support.
  • Open loops: preserve Princess-specific context and avoid duplicate task systems.
  • Next concrete move: use this page for Princess UX continuity.

Migrated source: concepts/princess-ux-work.md

Princess UX/UI Work

This page is the planning/context layer for Jenffry's Princess UX/UI work. Jira should remain the work execution/source-of-truth board once organized; TickTick should hold only concrete dated personal execution blocks; Sapho should help with triage, clarification, synthesis, and handoff preparation.

Current purpose

Help Jenffry keep Princess UX/UI work organized enough to execute calmly, communicate clearly, and use the work for senior UX growth rather than just task survival.

12-month senior UX success buckets

Jenffry endorsed five buckets as the Princess UX/career goal frame:

  • Work quality: cleaner flows, better systems thinking, stronger prototypes, better edge cases, stronger design decisions.
  • Execution: faster, more reliable, better at finishing, handoff, and ambiguity reduction.
  • Influence: more trust from PMs/devs/stakeholders and more leadership in conversations.
  • Portfolio / visible proof: 2–3 strong shipped or documented projects/case studies.
  • AI/work leverage: AI used for research, synthesis, design review, Jira clarity, and documentation without creating admin soup.

Current Princess work clusters from TickTick

Immediate / this week

  • Payment / booking failure handling — write team clarification questions before design starts. Focus: failure matrix, Manage Booking handoff, confirmation modal behavior, partial failures, and no-charge booking-creation failure.
  • FCC Balance / pricing transparency — get Sweeta context and capture Jenffry's info dump. Core issue: logged-in guest with FCC saw unclear price/balance/deposit behavior; engineering says FCC deduction was in Polar PMTS response but visible only via header dropdown.
  • Flexpay stage review — compare implementation against expected UX; capture bugs, gaps, and dev feedback.
  • Nearest Airport review — review at the 4 PM meeting and log feedback/follow-up actions afterward.
  • Confirmation Page updates — remove phone number, check whether removing cancellation link is possible, and surface Manage Booking link for all three booking scenarios.
  • Enriched Ports — revise icon direction and create shareable prototype for Jim.
  • Flexpay mobile summary — build mobile summary pages and add them to ready-for-dev file.
  • Flexpay guest info for other markets — complete before mid-May.

Organization / system work

  • Organize Jira — reflect all active Princess projects in Jira and make it the execution/source-of-truth board.
  • Mobile right rail behavior — define or confirm UX interaction spec: expansion, extent, open/close actions, and scroll behavior.

Career leverage / AI workflow

  • UX + AI workflow — gather practical ideas, turn them into repeatable workflows, test one on a real Princess task, and capture what worked.
  • Storybook/component states — define one Princess component's useful states.
  • Figma foundations / token extraction — audit whether color, type, spacing, radius, and component variables are ready for Figma → tokens → React prototype pipeline.
  • Research → design implications → prototype requirements template — create a reusable transformation template.
  • Princess-specific AI design guidelines — define rules for AI-assisted Princess design/prototype changes.

Operating structure

Use three layers:

  1. Jira — official work board and project status once organized.
  2. TickTick 💼Career list — Jenffry's personal dated execution blocks for work, Princess UX/UI, and business/career tasks.
  3. Sapho/wiki — context, prioritization, question lists, synthesis, templates, and recurring review signal.

2026-05-18 taxonomy update: the TickTick project with ID 69ffb6278f0818deaa8b8917 is now 💼Career. Princess UX/UI remains a work subtrack inside Career rather than its own top-level life-area list.

2026-05-09 update: Created dedicated TickTick list 💼Career (69ffb6278f0818deaa8b8917) and moved 20 active Princess-related tasks into it from 💼Career, including the former Princess-column items and Princess-specific UX + AI workflow items.

Current triage pattern

For each Princess item, Sapho should classify it as one of:

  • Clarify — missing decision, stakeholder question, technical constraint, or acceptance rule.
  • Design — actual Figma/prototype/UX output needed.
  • Review — compare stage/prototype/implementation against expected UX.
  • Handoff — document requirements, states, edge cases, or Jira-ready notes.
  • Systemize — convert repeated work into template, AI workflow, component state model, or design-system asset.

First recommended work sequence

  1. Clarify before designing: Payment booking failure + FCC Balance need questions/context first.
  2. Review committed items: Flexpay stage + Nearest Airport reviews should generate concrete Jira follow-ups.
  3. Finish small Confirmation Page batch: phone number, cancellation link feasibility, Manage Booking link.
  4. Consolidate in Jira: after the immediate clusters are understood, make Jira reflect the real project map.
  5. Use one real Princess task to test UX + AI workflow: do not keep AI workflow abstract.

Storybook design-system project progress

2026-05-14 research direction: Jenffry wants to explore creating a Storybook-based Princess Cruises design system because the current design-side system is incomplete. The goal is not merely a pretty component catalog; the useful version would become a higher-level coded/design-system reference that improves Princess work, senior UX leverage, AI-assisted design workflows, documentation, and handoff quality.

Recommended pilot scope

Start with a small Storybook pilot rather than the whole system:

  1. Minimal foundations: color, typography, spacing, radius.
  2. First components: Button, Input/Form Field, Alert/Notification.
  3. Then one real Princess pattern: Payment/booking failure alert + form state, mobile right rail, or payment summary.
  4. Include Figma references, states/variants, accessibility notes, and usage guidance.

Reusing an existing Storybook/design system

Initial feasibility assessment: reusing an existing open-source Storybook/design-system repo as a scaffold is possible and may be valuable, but only as a structural accelerator, not as a visual/UX source of truth. Candidate reference/scaffold sources researched include Storybook's own design-system repo, IBM Carbon, and React USWDS. These show mature patterns for component docs, tokens, Storybook stories, testing, and package structure.

Best use of an existing system:

  • copy/learn the repository structure, documentation pattern, story organization, testing setup, and token pipeline;
  • replace visual identity with Princess tokens and CSS values;
  • adapt only generic components whose behavior fits Princess needs;
  • treat complex Princess booking/payment patterns as custom work, not copied components.

Main caution: simply skinning a full external component library can create false completeness. It may look complete while preserving alien behavior, naming, accessibility assumptions, spacing logic, and interaction models. The project should only use an existing Storybook as scaffolding if it improves speed without masking missing Princess product decisions.

2026-05-14 scaffold comparison: three practical options

Comparison criteria for a Princess Storybook pilot: speed to useful prototype, fit for incomplete design foundations, ability to express Princess tokens/CSS, low false-completeness risk, accessibility/documentation quality, maintainability for a non-engineer-led UX effort, and usefulness as senior UX/career proof.

Option 1: Storybook Design System repo

Source: storybookjs/design-system.

Strengths:

  • Strong Storybook-native example from the Storybook/Chromatic ecosystem.
  • Includes real component stories, docs, package/distribution pattern, Emotion styling, React components, CI, and Chromatic usage.
  • Leaner than large enterprise systems, so easier to study as a scaffold.
  • MIT license and mature enough to be a usable reference.

Risks:

  • Visual/interaction choices are Storybook-branded, not Princess-branded.
  • It is not a generic starter; it is a real product design system for Storybook web properties.
  • Uses assumptions that may not match Princess implementation stack or Figma structure.

Best Princess use:

  • Use as structural reference: Storybook config, component/story organization, docs style, package setup, Chromatic/testing pattern.
  • Do not use as the actual Princess component source.

Assessment: best reference scaffold if the goal is a serious but still manageable coded design-system prototype.

Option 2: React USWDS

Source: trussworks/react-uswds.

Strengths:

  • Active React + TypeScript component library based on USWDS 3.0.
  • Good example of turning an external design standard into reusable React components with Storybook documentation.
  • Strong accessibility and government/public-service design-system influence.
  • Useful model for component APIs, prop documentation, variants, and standards alignment.

Risks:

  • Its design language is federal/government, not cruise/travel/ecommerce.
  • It depends on USWDS CSS/version alignment; this is an extra inherited constraint Princess does not need.
  • Reskinning it would likely preserve alien naming, markup, and behavior assumptions.

Best Princess use:

  • Use as reference for accessibility, component coverage, docs discipline, and how to implement a design standard in React.
  • Do not use as the base repo unless the goal is only to study structure.

Assessment: best reference for accessibility/documentation rigor, but weaker as a direct scaffold.

Option 3: Minimal Storybook + tokens starter

Source pattern: create a fresh React + Vite + Storybook project, then add a token pipeline and selected addons/tools. Relevant references include official Storybook React Vite docs, Style Dictionary for token builds, and small starter/addon repos.

Strengths:

  • Lowest false-completeness risk because every component added must be intentional.
  • Best fit for incomplete Princess design foundations: we can represent known, inferred, and proposed tokens separately.
  • Easier to align with Princess-specific components instead of inherited generic library assumptions.
  • Cleanest path for a personal/internal/portfolio pilot.

Risks:

  • Slower initial setup than cloning a mature repo.
  • Requires deciding folder conventions, token naming, docs structure, and first components.
  • Some starter repos are inactive or too small, so this should be assembled from current official tooling rather than copied wholesale.

Best Princess use:

  • Start fresh with React + Vite + Storybook.
  • Add Style Dictionary or plain CSS variables for tokens.
  • Copy only proven documentation/story patterns from Storybook Design System, React USWDS, and Carbon.
  • Build only the first 3 foundations/components plus one real Princess pattern.

Assessment: best actual starting method for Princess because it maximizes control and minimizes inherited design debt.

Working recommendation

Use Option 3 as the actual build path, with Option 1 and Option 2 as references:

  1. Create a clean minimal Storybook + React + Vite project.
  2. Add a lightweight token layer: CSS variables first, Style Dictionary later if useful.
  3. Borrow structure/docs/testing ideas from Storybook Design System.
  4. Borrow accessibility and component-state rigor from React USWDS.
  5. Keep Carbon as a reference for mature enterprise completeness, not as a base.
  6. Build a pilot around Button, Form Field/Input, Alert, and one real Princess booking/payment pattern.

Decision status: revised after Jenffry's speed concern. A clone-and-prune strategy is acceptable if treated as an audit-and-replacement workflow, not as a reskin. The test is whether replacing/correcting/deleting inherited pieces is faster than clean-building while still preserving Princess-specific judgment.

2026-05-14 clone-and-prune start

Jenffry chose to proceed with the Storybook Design System repo using a clone-and-prune strategy.

Local setup completed:

  • Cloned https://github.com/storybookjs/design-system.git into /opt/data/projects/princess-storybook-design-system.
  • Created working branch princess-clone-and-prune-spike.
  • Installed dependencies using npx --yes yarn@1.22.22 install --frozen-lockfile because global yarn is not installed and corepack enable cannot write symlinks to /usr/bin in this environment.
  • Verified static Storybook build with npx yarn@1.22.22 build-storybook; output generated at /opt/data/projects/princess-storybook-design-system/storybook-static.
  • Started local dev server at http://localhost:6006/ and verified HTTP 200 response.
  • Added local working note: /opt/data/projects/princess-storybook-design-system/PRINCESS_CLONE_AND_PRUNE.md.

Initial technical observations:

  • Repo uses Storybook 7.6.6, React 17, Emotion/Storybook theming, Webpack 5, Chromatic, and 46 story files.
  • Package license is stated as MIT in package.json; no separate LICENSE file was present in the clone, so attribution/license handling should be added before any publishable or portfolio-facing output.
  • Build warnings are mainly large-bundle warnings and outdated Browserslist data; not blockers for the pilot.

Clone-and-prune operating rule:

  • For inherited components, assign: keep, modify, replace, delete, or defer.
  • For Princess components/patterns, label evidence as: confirmed, adapted, inferred, proposed, or placeholder.

Next practical step: final decision made after reviewing Storybook Design System and considering the alternatives. Jenffry chose to build a custom Princess Storybook from scratch, using Storybook Design System, React USWDS, and Carbon as references/north stars rather than bases.

Decision rationale:

  • Storybook Design System is too small to serve as a useful base.
  • React USWDS and Carbon are useful references but carry too much external product/design philosophy to own directly.
  • The custom build should borrow the best patterns while keeping Princess product judgment, booking/payment patterns, naming, and token structure intentional.

Immediate implementation direction:

  1. Create a fresh Princess Storybook project instead of pruning the clone.
  2. Use React + Vite + Storybook as the likely base.
  3. Start with CSS variables/design tokens, then add Style Dictionary only if token complexity justifies it.
  4. Build the pilot around foundations, Button, Input/Form Field, Alert/Notification, and one Princess booking/payment failure pattern.
  5. Keep the three systems as active references:
  • Storybook Design System: docs/story structure and lightweight package organization.
  • React USWDS: accessibility, form rigor, and component API discipline.
  • Carbon: enterprise-level completeness and component/state coverage.

Operational cleanup completed:

  • Stopped the temporary Cloudflare public tunnel for the old clone.
  • Stopped the old Storybook dev server.
  • Paused the old clone backup job Princess Storybook GitHub backup (f1ea41a336d3) so it does not continue backing up the wrong base.

Access/backup update:

  • VPS public IP checked as 204.168.200.125.
  • Dev server process is running via npx yarn@1.22.22 storybook -- --host 0.0.0.0 at http://localhost:6006/ on the VPS.
  • Recommended safe viewing method from Jenffry's own computer is SSH local port forwarding rather than exposing Storybook publicly: ssh -L 6006:localhost:6006 <vps-user>@204.168.200.125, then open http://localhost:6006 locally.
  • Installed cloudflared locally at /opt/data/home/.local/bin/cloudflared and created a temporary public Cloudflare quick tunnel for sharing: https://asbestos-mil-fog-junior.trycloudflare.com; verified HTTP 200 response. This URL is public/unauthenticated and temporary; use only for low-risk sharing until a named/authenticated tunnel or static preview deployment is configured.
  • Created private GitHub backup repo: jenffry90-arch/princess-storybook-design-system.
  • Renamed original Storybook remote to upstream; set private repo as origin.
  • Pushed branch princess-clone-and-prune-spike with commit Start Princess clone-and-prune spike.
  • Created scheduled backup job Princess Storybook GitHub backup (f1ea41a336d3) every 30 minutes using /opt/data/home/scripts/princess_storybook_git_backup.sh; it stays silent when there are no changes and pushes committed changes when present.

Open Storybook questions

  • Is this a personal prototype, internal Princess work asset, or portfolio/career proof?
  • What stack should it use: React + Vite/Next, plain CSS variables, Tailwind, or the company's actual implementation stack?
  • Can Jenffry access the relevant Princess Figma foundations and component libraries?
  • Should the first build be scaffolded from an existing open-source design system or started clean with selected patterns copied manually?
  • Which first Princess component/pattern will prove value fastest?

Enriched Ports + Port Count Enhancement context

2026-05-15 synthesis from Jenffry: the Enriched Ports work and Port Count Enhancement work overlap and were combined.

Original Enriched Ports problem

The initial goal was to highlight itineraries that include scenic cruising.

During review, the team discovered that not all scenic cruising is the same:

  1. Scenic cruising / viewing experience
  • The ship passes by or through a meaningful location.
  • The guest experience is primarily looking/viewing.
  • No distinct onboard activity is attached.
  • This appears to be the majority of scenic cruising cases.
  1. Enriched cruising
  • A scenic cruising moment that has an activity attached to the viewing experience.
  • The activity makes it more than passive viewing.
  • This became the working definition for “enriched cruising.”

Design implication: scenic cruising was split into two categories because guest value and expectation differ:

  • scenic cruising = viewing-only experience;
  • enriched cruising = viewing experience plus attached activity.

Planned surfaces:

  • Search results/search card.
  • Itinerary page/itinerary timeline.

Port Count Enhancement problem

The goal is to portray ports/days in a way that better matches how guests think about an itinerary.

Key shift: stop describing only “ports”; instead describe the trip in day categories that add up or at least explain the itinerary more intuitively.

Working counting concepts:

  • Total itinerary days — the full length of the itinerary.
  • Destination days — count days with destinations, including embarkation/departure port and disembarkation/arrival port.
  • Sea days — ordinary cruising-at-sea days.
  • Scenic cruising days — viewing-focused scenic cruising days.
  • Enriched cruising days — scenic cruising days with an attached activity.
  • Land days — cruise-tour land portion days, counted separately from cruise days when relevant.

Embark/disembark counting rule:

  • If embarkation and disembarkation ports are different, count both as destination days.
  • If they are the same round-trip port, count them as one destination because they are the same port.

Key unresolved counting issue

Round trips create a math/communication problem:

  • Destination days + sea days + scenic/enriched cruising days may not equal total itinerary days if the same round-trip embark/disembark port is counted once rather than twice.
  • This needs either a counting rule, label, or explanatory copy so the numbers do not feel inconsistent or wrong.

Possible framing to explore:

  • Separate “unique destinations” from “destination days.”
  • Or count embarkation/disembarkation as itinerary days but show the same port once in the destination list.
  • Or use “ports visited” for unique places and “itinerary day breakdown” for day math.

Current design task

Organize the combined task around three questions:

  1. Taxonomy: What are the categories? Destination day, sea day, scenic cruising day, enriched cruising day, land day.
  2. Counting logic: What exactly gets counted in each category, especially embark/disembark and round trips?
  3. Display logic: How should search cards and itinerary pages show the value without confusing the guest?

Open questions for Jenffry

  • For Enriched Ports / Port Count Enhancement: should “destination days” mean unique destinations, calendar days with destination activity, or both as separate labels?
  • For round trips, should the same embark/disembark port be counted once for unique destination count but twice in the day breakdown?
  • Should scenic cruising and enriched cruising be shown as separate day categories, or should enriched cruising appear as a badge/detail inside scenic cruising?
  • What is the official Jira structure today: epics, tickets, loose board, or something else?
  • Which Princess projects are politically/highest-stakes this week?
  • Who are the key stakeholders for each active cluster: Jim, Sweeta, Washington/Guilherdes, engineers, PMs?
  • What does “organized” mean for Princess work: cleaner Jira, weekly priority map, Figma file cleanup, meeting prep, handoff templates, or all of these?
  • What recurring cadence would help without adding admin soup: Monday planning, end-of-day work capture, Friday work review, or only on-demand?

Finances

This page tracks Jenffry's finance and household-budget context.

Current financial context

  • Household is on one income.
  • Current regular income is $2,882.97 biweekly net.
  • Money is tight because fixed debt/loan obligations consume most regular income.
  • Budget planning must exclude tax refunds, interest, large deposits, reimbursements, transfers, and other one-off income.
  • April 2026 looked temporarily better because of IRS tax refund/credit entries, but those are not normal income.
  • Credit card interest/fees appeared in April and May; the budget should prevent interest from restarting.
  • Emergency/long-term savings context from 2026-05-11: Jenffry reported about $36k emergency fund and over $500k in 401k. The issue is monthly comfort/cashflow, not total net-worth panic.

Baseline income assumption

Use regular take-home pay only for everyday planning.

  • Biweekly net income: $2,882.97
  • Annualized net income: $74,957.22
  • Monthly average: $6,246.44
  • Weekly average: $1,441.49
  • Do not build everyday budget around tax refunds, reimbursements, transfers, interest, cash deposits, or one-off income.

Observed current-paycheck pattern in 2026 includes Princess Cruise deposits around the stated biweekly income. April 2026 IRS TREAS 310 entries of $8,501 and $1,495 are treated as one-off income and excluded from the budget baseline.

Structural constraint

Debt/loan payments dominate the current budget.

Current assumed debt/loan obligations:

  • JPMorgan Chase: $3,496.12/month
  • TD Auto Finance reserve: $466.86/month
  • Honda: likely paid off or at least uncertain after the large $12,259.70 payment on 2026-04-07; confirm whether any $298.31 Honda payment still continues.

JPMorgan + TD equals $3,962.98/month, leaving about $2,283.46/month for all non-debt household spending.

Realistic monthly budget — income-based

This is the working budget from the 2026-05-14 transaction review, with the dashboard budget adjusted on 2026-05-25 to make leisure more realistic while keeping the one-income constraint intact.

  • JPMorgan loan/debt: $3,496.12
  • TD Auto Finance reserve: $466.86
  • Bills & utilities: $650.00
  • Groceries: $650.00
  • Auto: gas/tolls/parking: $350.00
  • Dining & social: $150.00
  • Shopping / household: $125.00
  • Leisure: $100.00
  • Personal care: $50.00
  • Health / wellness / medical: $75.00
  • Pets: $50.00
  • Software / subscriptions: $50.00
  • Family / gifts / obligations: $33.44
  • Travel: $0.00
  • Fees / interest: $0.00
  • Misc / buffer: $0.00

Total budgeted: $6,246.42 against $6,246.44 monthly average income.

Mentat assessment: this budget is realistic because it respects the actual one-income constraint, but it is not comfortable. It has no meaningful buffer unless a debt payment disappears, income increases, or categories are cut further.

Weekly operating budget equivalent

JPMorgan loan/debt, TD Auto Finance reserve, and Bills & utilities are monthly obligations/reserves, not weekly spending categories. The weekly dashboard view is for controllable operating-spending pace only.

  • Groceries: $149.60
  • Auto: gas/tolls/parking: $80.55
  • Dining & social: $34.52
  • Shopping / household: $28.77
  • Leisure: $23.02
  • Personal care: $11.51
  • Health / wellness / medical: $17.26
  • Pets: $11.51
  • Software / subscriptions: $11.51
  • Family / gifts / obligations: $7.70
  • Travel: $0.00
  • Fees / interest: $0.00
  • Misc / buffer: $0.00

Weekly operating budget: about $375.94/week after monthly JPMorgan, TD, and Bills & utilities obligations/reserves.

May 2026 status through 2026-05-14

From the 2026-05-17 SimpleFIN refresh. Live transaction data currently runs through 2026-05-14, so spending after that date may not yet be reflected.

  • Actual May budget spending so far: $5,085.04
  • Non-debt May spending so far: $1,588.92
  • Remaining against monthly average income: $1,161.40
  • If reserving TD Auto Finance for later in May: real usable remaining amount is about $694.54
  • Practical remaining daily pace after TD reserve: about $40.86/day for the remaining 17 days after 2026-05-14, or about $285.99 for a 7-day stretch.

Category status:

  • JPMorgan loan/debt: $3,496.12 spent; $0 remaining
  • TD Auto Finance reserve: $0 spent; $466.86 should stay reserved
  • Bills & utilities: $368.15 spent; $281.85 remaining
  • Groceries: $270.25 spent; $379.75 remaining
  • Auto: gas/tolls/parking: $192.26 spent; $157.74 remaining
  • Dining & social: $42.50 spent; $107.50 remaining
  • Shopping / household: $188.92 spent; over by $13.92
  • Leisure: $124.15 spent; over by $74.15
  • Software / subscriptions: $65.83 spent; over by $15.83
  • Travel: $209.80 spent; over by $209.80
  • Fees / interest: $97.07 spent; over by $97.07

Week of 2026-05-08 through 2026-05-14:

  • Week spending: $488.05
  • Non-debt weekly budget: about $525.54
  • Remaining against weekly non-debt budget: about $37.49
  • Problem areas: leisure, auto, shopping, and software were over their weekly caps; bills are tracked monthly, while groceries and dining/social were controlled.

Budget rules

  1. Treat $525/week as the practical non-debt operating budget.
  2. For the rest of May 2026, treat about $720 as usable after reserving TD Auto Finance.
  3. No more travel spending in May unless unavoidable.
  4. No more leisure spending in May if avoidable unless it is intentional and inside the updated $100/month leisure cap.
  5. Shopping only for true household necessities until the month resets.
  6. Fees/interest are a leak to fix, not a normal category.
  7. Dining/social is allowed but modest; lunch with friends is acceptable if kept around $15–20.
  8. If Honda still continues as a monthly payment, revise the budget immediately because there is currently no room for it.
  9. Extra/irregular income order: emergency buffer, planned annual expenses, debt/large obligations, then fun.
  10. Do not restart automatic vault/savings transfers unless the month is already cash-positive.

12-month finance direction

Jenffry wants to move closer to financial freedom, but the current issue is monthly comfort and cashflow. The desired 12-month marker remains a comfortable budget with about $400/month excess after ordinary expenses, including room for enjoyment money.

Current 12-month success markers:

  • Emergency fund remains intact and not used for ordinary lifestyle leakage.
  • Credit-card interest does not restart.
  • Monthly budget works on regular income, not tax refunds, reimbursements, transfers, or one-off income.
  • There is roughly $400/month excess after normal expenses.
  • The budget includes some enjoyment money rather than being almost entirely survival money.
  • Weekly finance review, spending caps, monthly budget reset, and eventual automatic savings all support the system.

Transaction API integration

As of 2026-05-14, SimpleFIN Bridge is connected for read-only transaction access.

  • Secret storage: SIMPLEFIN_ACCESS_URL in /opt/data/.env.
  • Local importer: /opt/data/scripts/simplefin_import.py.
  • Current output folder: /opt/data/finance/simplefin/.
  • 2026-05-17 refresh succeeded: 15 accounts and 556 transactions returned; SimpleFIN may include a non-fatal range-cap warning.
  • Use the importer before finance reviews to refresh accounts.csv and transactions.csv.
  • Continuation task created in TickTick 💰Finances: Continue SimpleFIN transaction API setup, due 2026-05-18 at 9:00 AM America/New_York.

Security rule: do not save or print the SimpleFIN access URL in the wiki, memory, or final replies. Treat it like a financial-data credential even though access is read-only.

Forward assumptions to verify

Previous assumptions from earlier finance cleanup:

  • Claude subscription cancelled.
  • DoorDash DashPass cancelled.
  • Rocket Money cancelled.
  • Spotify cancelled.
  • Honda payment no longer active.
  • Prime is annual, not monthly.

The most important unresolved verification is whether Honda truly stopped after the April 2026 payoff-sized payment.

Migrated source: concepts/health.md

Health

This is the top-level life-area page for Health.

Subcategories

1. Mental health

Use mental-health for the mental-health goal layer: observer mode, acceptance/commitment work, therapy/Sapho support, and signs of grounded presence.

2. Physical health

Use physical-health for Hevy-backed body composition, strength, cardio, and medical-loop targets.

Use physical-health for practical routines: sleep, food, gym preparation, grooming, energy, body maintenance, and medical follow-through.

Working rule

Health is one life area with two subcategories: mental health and physical health. Physical health includes the practical body-system routines that make better weeks easier.

Hevy API data review — 2026-05-24

Purpose

Review what Sapho can retrieve from Jenffry's Hevy Pro API and how to use it for dashboards, weekly reviews, monthly reviews, and gym-planning feedback without adding TickTick clutter.

API source

  • Docs: https://api.hevyapp.com/docs/
  • API version in docs: 0.0.1
  • Status: early public API for Hevy Pro users; Hevy warns the API may change.
  • Auth pattern: api-key header. Do not print the key.
  • Local helper: /opt/data/scripts/hevy_api.py

Live account inventory checked

  • Workouts: 149 total, fetched across 15 pages.
  • Routines: 13 total, fetched across 2 pages.
  • Routine folders: 1 total.
  • Exercise templates: 436 total, fetched across 44 pages.
  • Body measurement entries: 3 total.
  • Workout events since 2026-01-01: available, 3 pages.

Endpoint categories available

User info

  • GET /v1/user/info
  • Data shape: user id, name, profile URL.
  • Use: identity check only. Low training value.

Workouts

  • GET /v1/workouts
  • GET /v1/workouts/count
  • GET /v1/workouts/events?since=...
  • GET /v1/workouts/{workoutId}
  • POST /v1/workouts
  • PUT /v1/workouts/{workoutId}

Observed workout fields:

  • id
  • title
  • description
  • routine_id
  • start_time
  • end_time
  • created_at
  • updated_at
  • exercises

Observed exercise-in-workout fields:

  • exercise_template_id
  • title
  • index
  • notes
  • sets
  • superset_id

Observed set fields:

  • index
  • type
  • weight_kg
  • reps
  • distance_meters
  • duration_seconds
  • rpe
  • custom_metric

Observed set metric coverage across current workout history:

  • Reps: 2,127 set values.
  • Weight: 2,026 set values.
  • Distance: 7 set values.
  • Duration: 3 set values.
  • Set types: normal 1,253; failure 443; dropset 296; warmup 142.

Best uses:

  • Weekly consistency: days trained, last workout, missed routine days.
  • Routine coverage: Push/Pull/Lower/Posture/Cardio balance.
  • Set volume by exercise and by routine.
  • Strength progression by exercise: weight, reps, estimated 1RM, best set, volume.
  • Cardio/treadmill progression when distance and duration are logged.
  • Workout duration and training density.
  • Historical trend charts.

Caution:

  • /v1/workouts pageSize max is 10.
  • Exercise titles can vary; use exercise_template_id for reliable matching.
  • Warmups should usually be excluded from strength-progress calculations.
  • Dropsets and failure sets should be tracked but not treated the same as normal working sets in every metric.

Workout events

  • GET /v1/workouts/events?since=...
  • Returns updates and deletes, newest to oldest.
  • Use: maintain a local cache without re-fetching all workouts every time.

Best use:

  • Build a local Hevy cache under /opt/data and update it incrementally before dashboards/check-ins.
  • Detect deleted or edited workouts so the dashboard does not silently drift.

Exercise history

  • GET /v1/exercise_history/{exerciseTemplateId}
  • Optional filters: start_date, end_date.

Observed history entry fields:

  • exercise_template_id
  • workout_id
  • workout_title
  • workout_start_time
  • workout_end_time
  • set_type
  • weight_kg
  • reps
  • distance_meters
  • duration_seconds
  • rpe
  • custom_metric

Observed top-used exercise histories are available when using template IDs found in actual workouts. The first generic template page can produce empty histories because those templates may not have been used.

High-value template IDs observed:

  • Triceps Pushdown: 93A552C6
  • Lateral Raise (Dumbbell): 422B08F1
  • Preacher Curl (Machine): 1E9A6B8E
  • Bicep Curl (Barbell): A5AC6449
  • Lat Pulldown (Cable): 6A6C31A5
  • Bench Press (Smith Machine): 0FBF7195
  • Iso-Lateral Row (Machine): AA1EB7D8
  • Incline Bench Press (Smith Machine): 3A6FA3D1
  • Pull Up: 1B2B1E7C
  • Bench Press (Barbell): 79D0BB3A
  • Chest Fly (Machine): 78683336
  • Pull Up (Assisted): 2C37EC5E
  • Shrug (Machine): 19A38071
  • Leg Extension (Machine): 75A4F6C4

Best uses:

  • Faster and cleaner per-exercise trend analysis than scanning all workouts.
  • Dashboard strength ladder for chosen anchor exercises.
  • Auto-detect stale exercises or exercises with enough data for trend confidence.

Routines

  • GET /v1/routines
  • GET /v1/routines/{routineId}
  • POST /v1/routines
  • PUT /v1/routines/{routineId}

Observed routine fields:

  • id
  • title
  • folder_id
  • created_at
  • updated_at
  • exercises

Observed routine exercise fields:

  • exercise_template_id
  • title
  • index
  • notes
  • rest_seconds
  • sets
  • superset_id

Current routines include:

  • Sapho Day 1 — Push + Sauna
  • Sapho Day 2 — Posture Cardio A
  • Sapho Day 3 — Pull + Sauna
  • Sapho Day 4 — Posture Cardio B
  • Sapho Day 5 — Lower + Core
  • Lower
  • Upper
  • Legs
  • Pull
  • Push
  • Rutina A
  • Rutina B
  • Rutina C

Best uses:

  • Canonical program definition.
  • Compare planned routine vs completed workouts.
  • Identify if completed workouts follow the current Sapho 5-Day plan or older routines.
  • Program audit: missing movement patterns, too much arm isolation, low lower-body frequency, missing cardio/posture days.

Caution:

  • Write actions should be verified by re-listing routines.
  • Routine creation/update is a side effect; do only when explicitly requested.

Routine folders

  • GET /v1/routine_folders
  • GET /v1/routine_folders/{folderId}
  • POST /v1/routine_folders

Observed folder fields:

  • id
  • title
  • index
  • created_at
  • updated_at

Use:

  • Mostly organization. Low analytical value.
  • Could help separate active Sapho plan from old routines.

Exercise templates

  • GET /v1/exercise_templates
  • GET /v1/exercise_templates/{exerciseTemplateId}
  • POST /v1/exercise_templates

Observed template fields:

  • id
  • title
  • type
  • equipment
  • primary_muscle_group
  • secondary_muscle_groups
  • is_custom

Best uses:

  • Muscle-group classification.
  • Equipment classification.
  • Stable exercise matching by ID.
  • Build dashboards by movement/muscle category.
  • Improve weekly reviews: not just routine titles, but actual trained areas.

Body measurements

  • GET /v1/body_measurements
  • GET /v1/body_measurements/{date}
  • POST /v1/body_measurements
  • PUT /v1/body_measurements/{date}

Observed fields:

  • date
  • weight_kg
  • neck_cm
  • shoulder_cm
  • chest_cm
  • left_bicep_cm
  • right_bicep_cm
  • left_forearm_cm
  • right_forearm_cm
  • waist
  • abdomen
  • hips
  • left_thigh
  • right_thigh
  • left_calf
  • right_calf
  • created_at
  • id

Current measurement coverage:

  • 2026-05-11: full body measurement set, except no explicit body-fat API field observed.
  • 2026-02-26: weight only.
  • 2024-07-31: weight only.

Best uses:

  • Body-composition dashboard.
  • Measurement trend lines when more entries are added.
  • Goal progress bars: current measurement on left, goal on right.
  • Weight trend.

Caution:

  • Body fat percentage was not observed as a Hevy body-measurement API field.
  • PUT /v1/body_measurements/{date} overwrites all fields; omitted fields become null. Never update measurements partially unless first fetching existing values and merging.

Current data pattern

Top historical workout titles:

  • Rutina B: 43
  • Rutina A: 39
  • Lunes y jueves: 24
  • Martes y viernes: 22
  • Rutina C: 11
  • Sapho Day 1/2/3/5 currently have 2 each.
  • Sapho Day 4 exists as a routine but had no observed completed workout in the latest fetched history at time of review.

Most frequent exercises in history:

  • Triceps Pushdown: 62 workout appearances.
  • Lateral Raise (Dumbbell): 59.
  • Preacher Curl (Machine): 58.
  • Bicep Curl (Barbell): 53.
  • Lat Pulldown (Cable): 39.
  • Bench Press (Smith Machine): 36.
  • Iso-Lateral Row (Machine): 32.
  • Incline Bench Press (Smith Machine): 31.
  • Pull Up: 29.
  • Bench Press (Barbell): 26.

Recent workouts checked:

  • 2026-05-22: Sapho Day 5 — Lower + Core.
  • 2026-05-20: Sapho Day 3 — Pull + Sauna.
  • 2026-05-19: Sapho Day 2 — Posture Cardio A.
  • 2026-05-18: Sapho Day 1 — Push + Sauna.
  • 2026-05-16: Sapho Day 5 — Lower + Core.
  • 2026-05-15: Sapho Day 3 — Pull + Sauna.
  • 2026-05-12: Sapho Day 2 — Posture Cardio A.
  • 2026-05-11: Sapho Day 1 — Push + Sauna.

Recommended Sapho usage model

Daily

Do not run a heavy Hevy review daily. Use Hevy only if Jenffry asks or if a dashboard refresh needs it.

Daily check-in should ask/track:

  • Today’s intended workout or substitution.
  • Whether workout happened.
  • Energy/sleep/mental capacity signal.

Weekly review

Use Hevy heavily.

Weekly review should include:

  • Workouts completed.
  • Last workout date.
  • Routine coverage.
  • Minimum / strong / ideal classification.
  • Missed routine day.
  • Best strength signal.
  • Next-week gym target.

Monthly review

Use Hevy for strategic health review:

  • 30-day consistency.
  • Routine balance.
  • Strength trend.
  • Body measurement changes.
  • Cardio trend.
  • Stale or overused patterns.
  • Next-month training focus.

Dashboard

Best Hevy dashboard uses:

  • 7-day / 30-day workout counts.
  • Lifting vs cardio/posture split.
  • Current Sapho routine coverage.
  • Strength ladder by anchor exercises.
  • Body measurement progress.
  • Treadmill mph conversion from distance + duration.
  • Last workout / next suggested workout.

Local cache

Build a local cache using:

  1. Initial full pull from /v1/workouts, /v1/routines, /v1/exercise_templates, /v1/body_measurements.
  2. Incremental updates from /v1/workouts/events?since=....
  3. Per-exercise histories for dashboard anchor exercises.

This avoids refetching all 149 workouts repeatedly and handles edits/deletes.

Biggest technical improvements to make next

Recommended next move: upgrade /opt/data/scripts/hevy_api.py.

Add these commands:

  1. inventory
  • Shows total workouts, routines, templates, measurements, and API coverage.
  1. cache-sync
  • Builds a local Hevy cache so we do not repeatedly pull all workouts.
  1. exercise-trends
  • Uses exercise_template_id for anchor-lift trends.
  1. weekly-review
  • Outputs the minimum / strong / ideal classification automatically.
  1. body-measurements
  • Gives clean dashboard-ready body measurement data.
  1. routine-coverage
  • Detects which Sapho plan days were hit or missed.

Decision notes

  • Hevy should remain the gym source of truth.
  • TickTick should not be used for workout details.
  • Dashboard and weekly/monthly reviews should consume Hevy data.
  • For future dashboard work, use stable Hevy exercise_template_id whenever possible; exercise title text is a fallback only.
  • Hevy API is good enough for a serious Sapho gym intelligence layer. The main limitation is not data access — it is building the right local cache and using stable exercise IDs instead of fragile title matching.
Mental Health
areas/health/mental-health.md
degree 21

Status: Active tracking system is now live through the final 15 TickTick mental-health habit trackers and the dashboard Health tab. Current priority: use the dashboard as the lightweight trend surface while keeping weekly review as the interpretation layer; av

Physical HealthRelationshipsLeisurePerception LogHealthProgress TrackingCurrent StrategyOpen Loops

Mental Health

Dashboard summary

  • Status: Active tracking system is now live through the final 15 TickTick mental-health habit trackers and the dashboard Health tab.
  • Current priority: use the dashboard as the lightweight trend surface while keeping weekly review as the interpretation layer; avoid turning daily tracking into rumination.
  • Active signals: Stop checking, Healthy diet, Mood, Sleep, Anxiety control, Energy, Walk/no-phone, Self reflect, Therapy, Physical activity, Social outing, Leisure activity, Life story, Relax, and Meditate.
  • Open loops: align Leisure and Relationships goals with outward-facing life without reducing them to mental-health tools; refine the Sapho mental-health support profile over time.
  • Next concrete move: use the first weekly review after the tracker launch to check whether these 15 signals are useful, too much, or missing one important signal.

Mental health is the lead stabilizing domain. Physical routines matter here when they support emotional capacity, morning energy, and future excitement, but detailed routine tracking belongs in physical-health. Leisure and relationships are separate life areas, but parts of them intentionally serve mental health by turning attention outward, restoring identity, and creating future excitement.

Mental-health SMART goal cascade

Long-term goal

Over the long term, I want to live as someone present, grounded, and committed to the actual life I have, with enough acceptance, outward attention, identity, and future excitement that fear no longer organizes my life.

Success looks like:

  • Fear, anxiety, intrusive thoughts, and checking can arise without becoming the center of the day.
  • My main operating procedure is: ignore checking, redirect attention, accept the feeling, and embrace that I can survive it.
  • I can feel anxiety, grief, fear, or intrusive thoughts without treating them as proof that my life is broken.
  • I am present in real activities: conversations, family, pickleball, hobbies, social settings, film/cinema, and work.
  • My identity feels repaired through action and social mirroring: people see my personality, interests, humor, taste, and presence in real life.
  • The broader life is good even if I do not feel fully “fixed.” Acceptance is part of success, not a consolation prize.

12-month goal

By 2027-05-24, I will feel more like myself again and stronger because of this chapter, as shown by a stable daily response to fear/checking, regular acceptance practice, active outward-focus hobbies/social life, and better presence in conversations, family, pickleball, and leisure.

12-month proof points:

  • I use the core strategy during normal days: ignore, redirect, accept, embrace.
  • Meditation or equivalent acceptance/emotional exposure practice happens most weeks, with a target of 20 minutes, 5 days per week when capacity allows.
  • I protect at least 1 outward-focus action per week through Leisure, Relationships, family, hobbies, or social life.
  • Intrusive thoughts and fear are handled as internal events, not commands or predictions.
  • I have at least one active identity/outward-focus channel — for example film club, cinema-related activity, RC racing, pickleball, windsurfing, social outings, or another hobby — that gets real attention outside my inner world.
  • I practice acceptance: even if anxiety returns or never fully disappears, life can still be good and livable.

6-month goal

By 2026-11-24, I will have a reliable mental-health operating loop: daily anti-checking response, 5-day acceptance/embrace practice target, weekly outward-focus action, and cross-links to Leisure and Relationships goals that support identity repair and future excitement.

6-month proof points:

  • The Sapho mental-health support profile exists: fear as the core mechanism, helpful responses, unhelpful responses, boundaries, and preferred language during spirals.
  • The weekly review includes a compact mental-health review: sleep/capacity trend, observer-mode/checking trend, acceptance practice follow-through, outward-focus action, and one adjustment.
  • A short anti-checking / observer-mode reset script exists and has been practiced.
  • Leisure goals define outward-focus hobbies, including whether film club belongs in the current cycle.
  • Relationships/social goals define intentional social exposure and identity repair without making relationships serve only mental health.
  • Tracking is useful but not obsessive: checkmarks and weekly review are preferred; 1–5 scores are supporting signals, not a self-worth grade.

Current monthly goal — through 2026-06-30

By 2026-06-30, I will establish the first SMART mental-health practice loop: use “ignore + redirect” as my daytime operating procedure, complete acceptance/embrace meditation on 20 days, and complete at least 4 outward-focus actions connected to hobbies, social life, family, or leisure.

Monthly proof points:

  • Daytime operating procedure: when checking/anxiety appears, use “I’m fine, I don’t need to check,” then redirect attention to a task, person, or hobby.
  • Acceptance/embrace practice: meditate or sit with the feeling for 20 minutes, 5 days per week, with 3 sessions/week as the minimum acceptable floor, aiming for 20 sessions by June 30.
  • Acceptance content: during practice, include both:
  • “I don’t have the life I wanted, but I have a very good life,” then name 3 things I am grateful for.
  • “If the bad feeling comes back, that is okay. I can deal with it. I will be fine. It may return or never fully go away, and I can still live.”
  • Outward-focus actions: complete 4 total outward-focus actions this month, using any Leisure/Relationships/Family source. No specific activity target is required. Examples: film club exploration, RC racing, pickleball, windsurfing step, movie/cinema activity, social outing, family outing, gym as energy builder.
  • Identity/social repair: capture at least 1 social or relationship action that lets personality be seen in real life, while leaving the detailed social/relationship goals to relationships and leisure.
  • Tracking: use the final 15 TickTick habit trackers as the lightweight data layer. Score habits track 7-day and 30-day averages; completion habits track 7-day and 30-day completion rates. Weekly review interprets the pattern; daily notes are evidence, not a demand for daily analysis.

Current weekly goal

This week, I will use the final mental-health tracker set to build a clean baseline: score the six daily signals, complete the daily grounding/reflection habits when useful, and protect at least one social/leisure/physical/therapy/life-story action.

Weekly proof:

  • Daily score signals: Stop checking, Healthy diet, Mood, Sleep, Anxiety control, and Energy have enough entries to produce a useful 7-day average.
  • Daily completion signals: Walk/no-phone and Self reflect are used as behavior checkmarks, not as mood autopsies.
  • Weekly completion signals: Therapy, Physical activity, Social outing, Leisure activity, and Life story each get one useful entry when they actually happen or are intentionally practiced.
  • Practice frequency: Relax reaches at least 3/week; Meditate aims for 5/week.
  • Weekly review asks: which signal predicted a better day, which habit was noise, and what should repeat next week?

Daily move

Today, I will complete the lightweight TickTick mental-health check-ins once, then use the data to move outward rather than keep checking myself.

Daily practice menu:

  1. Ignore checking: “I’m fine. I don’t need to check.”
  2. Redirect attention: put focus into a task, person, hobby, gym, family moment, or concrete next action.
  3. Accept: sit with the feeling without trying to escape it; name 3 things I am grateful for in the actual life I have.
  4. Embrace: invite the feared feeling to come back and do its worst; remind myself I can deal with it and still be fine.

Observer-mode reset script

Use this when checking, fear, observer mode, or intrusive thoughts start pulling attention inward. Keep it short; the goal is to exit the loop, not analyze the loop.

  1. Name it once: “This is fear/checking. I do not need to solve it right now.”
  2. Ignore the checking demand: “I’m fine. I don’t need to check.”
  3. Redirect outward: choose one visible next object of attention — task, person, family moment, work step, gym movement, hobby, movie, or physical environment.
  4. If the feeling stays: “It can stay. I can carry it and still live this moment.”
  5. Micro-action: do one concrete 2-minute outward action before reassessing anything.

Boundaries:

  • Do not repeat the script more than twice in a row; after that, move the body or change the environment.
  • Do not use the reset as a new way to check whether anxiety is gone.
  • Save deeper acceptance/embrace work for the 20-minute practice window.

Core problem targets

The current SMART targets cover these mental-health problem areas:

  1. Anxiety / feeling unsafe internally.
  2. Observer mode / constant checking.
  3. Intrusive thoughts.
  4. Low future excitement.
  5. Identity repair / feeling like yourself again.
  6. Acceptance / not resisting how life feels.
  7. Sleep / morning energy.
  8. Presence in conversations, family, pickleball, and hobbies.
  9. Social isolation / not enough people contact.

Fear is the core mechanism behind much of the pattern. The operating goal is not to eliminate fear on command; it is to stop fear from becoming the authority over attention, identity, and the future.

Core strategy

Daytime operating procedure

Use during normal life when checking, anxiety, observer mode, or intrusive thoughts appear:

  1. Ignore: “I’m fine. I don’t need to check.”
  2. Redirect: turn attention toward the outside world — task, person, hobby, family, work, gym, movie, activity, or next concrete action.

This is the main procedure throughout the day. It is deliberately simple because complicated analysis can become more checking.

Deliberate acceptance / embrace practice

Use in a bounded practice window, not all day:

  1. Accept: feel what is present instead of escaping, avoiding, distracting, or proving it away.
  2. Reframe actual life: “I don’t have the life I wanted, but I have a very good life.” Name 3 things I am grateful for.
  3. Embrace fear: the bad feeling may return; it may never fully go away; I can deal with it and still be fine.
  4. Re-enter life: end the practice by doing one concrete outward-facing action.

Target: 20 minutes per day, 5 days per week when capacity allows.

Current distress patterns

Anxiety, low future-excitement, intrusive thoughts

Jenffry identified a possible root pattern: when his mental health starts to feel worst, the source is usually anxiety. This appears linked with a lack of excitement about the future and the presence of intrusive thoughts.

Working interpretation, not diagnosis:

  • Anxiety may be the upstream driver behind many spikes.
  • Low future-excitement may make anxiety feel more trapped or totalizing.
  • Intrusive thoughts are part of the distress pattern and should be noticed without treating them as commands, predictions, or proof.
  • Useful response is likely not deeper rumination, but grounding plus recommitment to a concrete future-building action.

Observer mode / constant checking

Jenffry described a distressing pattern he calls “observer mode,” possibly dissociation-like. He later identified a more specific mechanism: because of the weed-related trauma plus underlying depression, anxiety, and stress, he may be constantly checking whether he is okay. That checking itself becomes a major source of distress and pulls him out of living in the moment.

The pattern includes:

  • looking into life from an outside observer perspective
  • zooming in on his own feelings and internal state
  • overthinking and over-analyzing how he feels
  • drawing broad, sweeping, all-encompassing conclusions from moment-to-moment feelings
  • becoming too aware of minute details of his own life

Desired movement:

  • more in the moment
  • more grounded
  • less stopped by overthinking
  • less dissociation-like distance from his life
  • less analysis of the fact that he is analyzing

Global life-story framing

Jenffry identified that repeated global wording such as “my life is over” may have deepened the distress pattern. The useful correction is not to deny painful specifics, but to stop letting one painful chapter define the whole life story.

Working interpretation, not diagnosis:

  • The mind can treat repeated all-encompassing language as evidence that the whole future is closed.
  • A better frame is specific and bounded: this chapter was painful, unwanted, and complicated; it does not prove that the whole life is ruined.
  • Writing Jenffry's life story may serve as a global reframe: preserve truth, but make room for future chapters, fatherhood, health, relationship clarity, ambition, and excitement.

Acceptance and non-resistance

Acceptance is not giving up. It is the protection against the “what if I do all this and it does not work?” loop.

Useful acceptance frame:

  • Healing work matters.
  • Also, life must be livable and good even if the feeling does not disappear on schedule.
  • Resisting the possibility of anxiety returning can restart the same fear/checking loop.
  • Acceptance means: this is life, this is what is here, and it can still be good.

Cross-links to other life areas

Leisure / future excitement

The future-excitement and hobbies portion of mental health will be filled through leisure. Candidate outward-focus channels include film club, RC racing, pickleball, windsurfing, movies/cinema writing, social outings, family outings, and gym as an energy builder.

Film club belongs primarily in Leisure, but it is especially relevant to mental health because it combines social exposure, identity repair, hobby focus, and outward attention.

Relationships / social identity repair

The social and identity-repair portion of mental health will connect to relationships and leisure. The mental-health use is intentional: relationships and social exposure can help Jenffry feel seen as a person again. But those areas should also stand on their own beyond mental health.

Tracking method

Preferred default: TickTick habit check-ins + weekly review, not daily deep scoring or notes analysis. The dashboard is the trend surface; Sapho's weekly/monthly reviews are the interpretation layer.

Current TickTick source-of-truth habits:

  • Daily 1–5 + note:
  • Stop checking
  • Healthy diet
  • Mood
  • Sleep
  • Anxiety — Option B anxiety control, 1 poor control to 5 strong control.
  • Energy
  • Daily note:
  • Walk, no phone
  • Self reflect
  • Weekly note, at least once/week:
  • Therapy
  • Physical activity
  • Social outing
  • Leisure activity
  • Life story
  • Practice frequency + note:
  • Relax — at least 3 times/week.
  • Meditate — at least 5 times/week.

TickTick API note: the public dashboard uses the final 15 habit trackers plus TickTick Habit Log emoji scores/notes. Raw Habit Log note content is written to /opt/data/wiki/private/health/mood-tracking-notes.md and is intentionally included in the public dashboard publisher.

Support implications for Sapho

Helpful Sapho stance:

  • recognize fear as the core pattern when it appears
  • distinguish facts, feelings, interpretations, and sweeping conclusions
  • avoid feeding circular self-monitoring
  • help Jenffry return to the daytime operating procedure: ignore checking and redirect attention
  • reserve acceptance/embrace work for bounded practice windows unless Jenffry explicitly wants to process
  • use acceptance-and-commitment framing: “this can be hard and still survivable; what life do you choose to commit to today?”
  • treat mental health as the top priority, while keeping family, body, work, leisure, and relationships connected to recovery

Avoid:

  • excessive interrogation of internal states when Jenffry is already in observer mode
  • overlong analysis that encourages more rumination
  • acting like therapy, diagnosis, or medical authority
  • pretending dashboard numbers can fully measure mental recovery
  • making safety/escalation discussion the center when Jenffry says he knows how to handle it, while still using ordinary emergency judgment if he ever reports danger or inability to stay safe

Dashboard / data rules

  • Mental-health dashboard signals should follow the final TickTick habit tracker list: Stop checking, Therapy, Physical activity, Healthy diet, Walk no phone, Social outing, Mood, Sleep, Anxiety, Energy, Relax, Leisure activity, Life story, Self reflect, and Meditate.
  • Use daily signals as trend context, not as a demand for analysis. Habit Log notes can be reviewed weekly/monthly.
  • Weekly review is the main interpretation layer: what helped, what worsened checking, what should repeat, and what should be dropped.
  • Perception dumps stay raw in perception-log unless Jenffry explicitly asks to process them.

Related pages

  • physical-health
  • progress-tracking
  • perception-log

  • relationships
  • leisure
Physical Health
areas/health/physical-health.md
degree 17

Status: Active support system for body composition, strength, cardio capacity, sleep/energy, and medical follow-through; system build marked complete on 2026-05-24 and now in normal review/calibration mode. Current priority: serve the dashboard and goals layer

Current StrategyOpen LoopsDashboardProgress Tracking

Physical Health

Dashboard summary

  • Status: Active support system for body composition, strength, cardio capacity, sleep/energy, and medical follow-through; system build marked complete on 2026-05-24 and now in normal review/calibration mode.
  • Current priority: serve the dashboard and goals layer first: keep current body measurements, Hevy workout rhythm, strength anchors, and the numeric goal ladder clear and source-backed.
  • Active signals: Hevy 7-day/30-day workouts and sets, routine balance, weight, waist, abdomen, body-fat estimate, key lift anchors, treadmill/cardio capacity, sleep/energy, testosterone/lipids follow-up.
  • Open loops: update body measurements monthly; keep strength anchors mapped to stable Hevy exercise IDs; avoid TickTick workout clutter; add a consistent body-fat method only if it helps rather than creates anxiety/admin load.
  • Next concrete move: keep Hevy as the physical-health source of truth and review body/strength trends weekly/monthly against the numeric cascade below.

Physical health contains gym, sleep, food, body maintenance, grooming, medical follow-up, and physical capacity. Hevy is the gym and body-measurement source of truth. The dashboard displays this page; it should not invent separate physical-health goals.

Current baseline from Hevy — 2026-05-11

Profile context:

  • Age: 35
  • Height: 6'4"
  • Current weight: 97.8 kg / 215.6 lb
  • Baseline weight for dashboard progress: 220 lb
  • BMI: about 26.2 — useful only as a rough population marker, not a body-composition diagnosis.

Current measurements:

  • Neck: 16.3 in
  • Shoulders: 25.4 in
  • Chest: 45.4 in
  • Left/right bicep: 14.3 / 14.5 in
  • Left/right forearm: 11.8 / 11.8 in
  • Abdomen: 39 in
  • Waist: 37 in
  • Body fat estimate: 26%
  • Hips: 44.1 in
  • Left/right thigh: 26.5 / 27.3 in
  • Left/right calf: 15.7 / 15.9 in

Physical-health goal cascade

Long-term goal

By 2028-05-22, I will have a lean, muscular, healthy build with lower abdominal fat and maintained or increased muscle.

Long-term targets:

  • Weight: 203 lb
  • Waist: 34.0 in
  • Abdomen: 35.0 in
  • Body fat estimate: 15%
  • Chest: 46.5 in
  • Shoulders: 26.5 in
  • Neck: 16.1 in
  • Hips: 44.0 in
  • Left bicep: 15.5 in

Strength tracking exercises

These are the exercises the dashboard should use in the strength tracking section. The dashboard should prefer stable Hevy exercise_template_id values and use exact titles only as fallback.

  • Bench press — 205 x 5
  • Shoulder press dumbbell — 100 x 10
  • Triceps pushdown — 120 x 10
  • Pull up — 12 unassisted pull-ups across 3–5 sets
  • Bicep curl — 95 x 10
  • Leg press — 550 x 8
  • Romanian deadlift — 225 x 5
  • Running / treadmill — 6.0 mph

Recent-current strength anchors from Hevy:

  • Bench press: baseline 135 x 10; current best from Hevy uses Bench Press (Barbell); goal 205 x 5.
  • Shoulder press dumbbell: baseline 50 x 10; current best from Hevy uses Shoulder Press (Dumbbell); goal 100 x 10.
  • Triceps pushdown: baseline 60 x 10; current best from Hevy uses Triceps Pushdown; goal 120 x 10.
  • Pull up: baseline 6; current best from Hevy uses Pull Up, not Assisted Pull-up; goal 12 unassisted pull-ups across 3–5 sets.
  • Bicep curl: baseline 60 x 10; current best from Hevy uses Bicep Curl (Barbell); goal 95 x 10.
  • Leg press: baseline 360 x 10; current best from Hevy uses Leg Press (Machine); goal 550 x 8.
  • Romanian deadlift: baseline 135 x 6; current best from Hevy uses Romanian Deadlift (Barbell); goal 225 x 5.
  • Treadmill: baseline 1.8 mph; current best from Hevy uses Treadmill; goal 6.0 mph. Hevy stores this as distance in meters plus duration in seconds, and the dashboard converts it to mph.

Hevy dashboard anchor IDs:

  • Bench Press (Barbell): 79D0BB3A
  • Shoulder Press (Dumbbell): 878CD1D0
  • Triceps Pushdown: 93A552C6
  • Pull Up: 1B2B1E7C
  • Bicep Curl (Barbell): A5AC6449
  • Leg Press (Machine): C7973E0E
  • Romanian Deadlift (Barbell): 2B4B7310
  • Treadmill: 243710DE

Practice layer

  • Weekly: review Hevy 7-day workout count, routine balance, minimum/strong/ideal classification, and next-week gym target in the weekly review.
  • Monthly: update weight/waist/abdomen/chest/arms/thighs/calves in Hevy and review body trend against strength performance.
  • Quarterly: reassess whether the 12-month strength targets are too aggressive, too easy, or causing pain/recovery problems.
  • Daily: do not duplicate workouts into TickTick. Daily planning may name gym intention/completion/substitution only.

Data cautions

  • Some older Hevy entries appear unsuitable for target-setting, especially obvious outliers such as a Smith-machine bench entry around 1140 lb. Use recent-current anchors, not all-time raw PRs, for goal extrapolation.
  • Body-fat percentage is not directly measured in Hevy yet. Use waist/abdomen trend, photos, strength, and weight as observable proxies unless a consistent body-fat measurement method is added.
  • Dashboard physical-health goals are display outputs. This page owns the numeric targets; the direct refresh script owns mechanical Hevy/source syncing; weekly/monthly reviews own interpretation.

Preserved historical material

Older migrated notes from concepts/physical-health.md and concepts/health-routines.md were moved out of the active page because they contained stale or conflicting targets. They are preserved at /opt/data/wiki/raw/reference/physical-health-migrated-material-2026-05-24.md.
